items.text: High above the city, on a tall column, stood the statue of the Happy Prince. He was gilded all over with thin leaves of fine gold, for eyes he had two bright sapphires, and a large red ruby glowed on his sword-hilt.
items.context: The opening of Wilde's fairy tale 'The Happy Prince,' introducing the central image and setting for a story about compassion and sacrifice. Published as the first tale in The Happy Prince and Other Tales.
items.publicReaction: The story became one of Wilde's most beloved works, praised for its poetic language and moral beauty despite—or because of—its tragic ending.
items.source: The Happy Prince and Other Tales, published 1888

items.text: Education is an admirable thing, but it is well to remember from time to time that nothing that is worth knowing can be taught.
items.context: Statement from The Critic as Artist, expressing Wilde's conviction that true knowledge and understanding come from individual experience and contemplation rather than instruction.
items.publicReaction: Became influential in aesthetic and modernist education theory; challenged Victorian assumptions about pedagogy.
items.source: The Critic as Artist, published in Intentions

items.text: Life is terribly deficient in form. Its catastrophes happen in the wrong way and to the wrong people.
items.context: Gilbert's observation in The Critic as Artist contrasting art's perfection with life's chaotic imperfection, demonstrating Wilde's philosophical position on the superiority of aesthetic form.
items.publicReaction: Became a famous articulation of the aesthetic movement's critique of everyday reality.
items.source: The Critic as Artist, published in Intentions

items.text: The proper aim of Art is the telling of beautiful untrue things.
items.context: Vivian's assertion in The Decay of Lying that aesthetic beauty supersedes factual accuracy as the goal of artistic creation.
items.publicReaction: Became a cornerstone statement of aesthetic philosophy; cited by both supporters and critics of the movement.
items.source: The Decay of Lying: A Dialogue, published in Intentions
